OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior iliac bones (pelvis).  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with luxation (knees).  
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (elbows).  
- Hypoplastic fingernails (clinical exam).  
- Age and context: 46-year-old male with HIV and hepatitis C coinfection; liver hemangiomas incidentally found but unrelated to bone findings.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Nailâpatella syndrome  
- Pros: Classic triad includes nail dysplasia (including hypoplastic fingernails), patellar hypoplasia or aplasia, and elbow and knee joint anomalies (including radial head hypoplasia and dislocations). Bilateral iliac spurs can also be seen.  
- Cons: None significant; findings fit well.  

2) CoffinâSiris syndrome  
- Pros: Nail dysplasia is common.  
- Cons: Main features include hypertrichosis, intellectual disability, and distinctive facial features; skeletal anomalies are usually of the distal phalanges rather than patella or radial head. Iliac spurs and patellar hypoplasia are not characteristic.  

3) MeierâGorlin syndrome  
- Pros: Short stature, patellar aplasia.  
- Cons: Usually presents with microtia, growth retardation, and characteristic facial features; nail hypoplasia is not a typical feature; bilateral iliac spurs are not reported.  

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome  
- Pros: Patellar aplasia/hypoplasia and limb anomalies.  
- Cons: Typically includes severe developmental delay, distinctive facial anomalies, and genital abnormalities. The presence of hypoplastic fingernails is not typical. Also, is very rare and usually fatal early.  

5) Genitopatellar syndrome  
- Pros: Patellar aplasia/hypoplasia, limb anomalies, genital anomalies.  
- Cons: Severe developmental delay, midface hypoplasia, and genitourinary anomalies; not characterized by nail hypoplasia or bilateral iliac spurs.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Nail-patella syndrome (matches all key findings: nail hypoplasia, patellar hypoplasia, radial head hypoplasia, iliac spurs).  
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome (patellar involvement and limb anomalies, but lacking nail hypoplasia and iliac spurs).  
3) Genitopatellar syndrome (similar to Coxopodopatellar but less match in nail findings).  
4) MeierâGorlin syndrome (patellar aplasia but lacks nail hypoplasia and iliac spurs).  
5) CoffinâSiris syndrome (nail hypoplasia present but skeletal findings not typical).  

Therefore, the findings are most consistent with Nailâpatella syndrome.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Nailâpatella syndrome  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base. Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed. It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe. A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Elderly male with new-onset orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea.  
- Elevated troponins and third-degree AV block.  
- Echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.  
- PET-CT shows large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue involving right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove, sparing left ventricle.  
- Hypermetabolic lung nodule at left lung base.  
- Contrast-enhanced CT confirms infiltrative hypodense myocardial mass on right side, with right coronary artery encirclement, moderate pericardial effusion.  
- Histologic diagnosis pending from lung nodule biopsy (but no results given yet).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Angiosarcoma:  
   - Pros: Most common primary malignant cardiac tumor; typical location is right atrium; can infiltrate myocardium and cause pericardial effusion; can be PET-avid.  
   - Cons: Usually aggressive; may present with hemorrhagic pericardial effusion; lung metastases possible (nodular image present).  

2) Ig-G4 related disease:  
   - Pros: Can cause pericardial thickening and mild infiltration; often infiltrative lesions.  
   - Cons: Usually inflammatory, not typically hypermetabolic on PET to this extent; usually affects multiple fibrotic sites; does not typically cause large myocardial masses or nodular lung lesions.  

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma:  
   - Pros: Can cause right atrial/ventricular masses; hypermetabolic on PET; can present with constitutional symptoms and conduction abnormalities; pericardial effusion typical. Lung nodules can represent metastases or synchronous lymphoma.  
   - Cons: Relatively rare; lymphoma tends to cause homogeneous masses, but hypermetabolic lesions fit.  

4) Erdheim-Chester disease:  
   - Pros: Can involve the myocardium with pericardial thickening and right atrial infiltration; PET-avid.  
   - Cons: Usually affects the right atrium and pericardium but often with systemic findings, like bone pain, long-bone sclerosis; lung nodules may be part; however, hypermetabolic myocardial tissue that spares left ventricle is consistent but less common.  

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma:  
   - Pros: Can arise in cardiac chambers, usually left atrium or ventricles; infiltrative masses; can cause pericardial effusion; can be PET-avid.  
   - Cons: Rare; usually on left side; less typical for right-sided infiltration sparing left ventricle; lung metastasis nodules plausible.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Primary cardiac lymphoma: Fits right atrium/right ventricle involvement, hypermetabolic mass on PET, lung nodule as metastatic site or synchronous disease; conduction abnormalities also common.  
2) Angiosarcoma: Also fits right atrium involvement, hypermetabolic mass, lung metastasis, pericardial effusion.  
3) Erdheim-Chester disease: Possible, but lung nodules less typical and left ventricle sparing is less specific.  
4) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma: Less likely due to right-sided predominance and typical left-sided location.  
5) Ig-G4 related disease: Least likely given PET avidity and mass effect.  

Given the right-sided cardiac mass with high PET uptake, conduction block, pericardial effusion, and lung nodule, the most likely diagnosis is primary cardiac lymphoma, especially with biopsy planned from lung nodule to support systemic disease.  
</analysis>  
<final> Primary cardiac lymphoma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus. model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 37-year-old man with high-grade fever, chills, vomiting, and epigastric abdominal pain.
- Labs show raised TLC (18,500 cells/Î¼L) with neutrophilia and mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase.
- Imaging: USG shows hepatomegaly and a large left lobe hepatic abscess (~350 cc) with impending rupture.
- Doppler and CECT show portal and/or portal-splenic vein thrombosis with cavernous transformation at the porta hepatis.
- Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts with obstruction distal to the common hepatic duct on MRCP.
- Intrahepatic biliary dilatation and intrahepatic venous collaterals.
- Pancreatic parenchymal varices noted draining into the splenic vein.
- Pinpoint narrowing of IVC with segmental thrombus.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1. Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation:
   - Pros: Presence of portal vein thrombosis and cavernous transformation; intrahepatic venous collaterals suggest portal hypertension; portal hypertension can cause varices formation.
   - Cons: No history or imaging findings specifically indicating liver cirrhosis (e.g., nodular liver surface, splenomegaly) mentioned. Hepatic abscess is atypical for cirrhosis without risk factors. Also, pancreatic parenchymal varices are not typical in cirrhosis-related portal hypertension.

2. Arteriovenous malformation (AVM):
   - Pros: No direct pros; could theoretically cause vascular issues.
   - Cons: No imaging findings showing direct evidence of AVMs (e.g., tortuous vessels, early venous filling). The imaging shows thrombosis and abscess, not AVM.

3. Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess:
   - Pros: Clearly matches clinical and imaging findings. Pylephlebitis (infected portal vein thrombosis) commonly associated with intraabdominal sepsis such as hepatic abscess. Imaging shows portal vein thrombosis and cavernous transformation, as well as a large hepatic abscess. Pancreatic parenchymal varices draining into splenic vein align with collateral formation secondary to portal hypertension due to pylephlebitis. Biliary obstruction distal to CHD also consistent with local inflammatory process.
   - Cons: None; this fits the scenario well.

4. Cystic lesions of pancreas:
   - Pros: Pancreatic involvement noted.
   - Cons: No cystic lesion described in pancreas. Findings include varices in pancreatic parenchyma rather than cysts.

5. Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMN):
   - Pros: Can cause pancreatic duct dilation and sometimes thrombosis if complicated.
   - Cons: No mention of ductal dilatation or mucinous cystic lesions; no imaging evidence of IPMN described. Also, no direct pancreatic lesion to explain abscess or porto-splenic thrombosis.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
- #3 Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess: Strongest fit with both clinical and imaging findings (hepatic abscess, portal vein thrombosis, cavernous transformation, pancreatic varices).
- #1 Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation: Less likely as no cirrhosis features described, and pancreatic varices not typical.
- #2 AVM: No imaging evidence.
- #4 Cystic lesions of pancreas: No imaging evidence.
- #5 IPMN: No imaging evidence.

Final decision: Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess.
</analysis>

<final>Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young adult male (31 years old) with right-sided scrotal swelling and voluminous solid mass.  
- Ultrasound shows a large heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic/necrotic components, vascularized, with no visible normal testicular or epididymal tissue on the right.  
- Normal left testis and epididymis.  
- Extremely high alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) >53,000 µg/L.  
- No metastases or lymphadenopathy on CT.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Mixed germinal cell tumour  
   - Pros: Common testicular tumour in this age group. Usually shows heterogeneous mass with solid/cystic components. AFP can be elevated, especially with yolk sac components.  
   - Cons: AFP usually elevated in mixed tumors but the level here is extremely high (>53,000 µg/L) which is more typical of pure yolk sac tumour; mixed tumors often have other markers (beta-hCG) or less massive AFP elevation.  

2) Pure yolk sac tumour  
   - Pros: Typical age group (young adult males), often presents as large heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic/necrotic components. Markedly elevated AFP is characteristic (often >10,000 µg/L). Ultrasound findings consistent.  
   - Cons: None significant; very high AFP strongly favors this diagnosis.  

3) Seminoma  
   - Pros: Common testicular tumour in this age group, usually homogeneous solid mass.  
   - Cons: AFP is typically normal or slightly elevated (AFP is usually normal). High AFP and heterogeneous mass with cystic/necrotic areas argue against seminoma. 

4) Choriocarcinoma  
   - Pros: Can present with elevated beta-hCG rather than AFP.  
   - Cons: AFP is usually normal or low. Elevated AFP argues against this diagnosis; also choriocarcinoma tends to be hemorrhagic and highly vascular but AFP is not a marker. 

5) Teratoma  
   - Pros: Can present as heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components.  
   - Cons: AFP is not typically markedly elevated; usually mixed germ cell tumour with teratomatous elements may have slightly raised AFP but not at this extreme level. No indication of characteristic mature or immature tissue on imaging.  

6) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can present with bilateral or unilateral testis masses, often homogeneous.  
   - Cons: Patient is a young adult (lymphoma more common older patients), AFP is not elevated, and mass is heterogeneous with cystic/necrotic areas and no normal testis seen, arguing against lymphoma. This is more typical of germ cell tumour.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pure yolk sac tumour - AFP >53,000 µg/L is pathognomonic and fits clinical and imaging features.  
2) Mixed germinal cell tumour - possible but typically AFP not as high, and no other clinical markers of mixed elements (beta-hCG).  
3) Seminoma - unlikely due to AFP and heterogeneous cystic necrotic components.  
4) Teratoma - less likely given AFP elevation and lack of detailed imaging features of teratoma.  
5) Choriocarcinoma - AFP not typical marker.  
6) Lymphoma - does not fit age or AFP level.  

Given the extremely high AFP and imaging features, pure yolk sac tumour is the most likely diagnosis.  
</analysis>  
<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

   - Cons: AFP usually elevated in mixed tumors but the level here is extremely high (>53,000 µg/L) which is more typical of pure yolk sac tumour; mixed tumors often have other markers (beta-hCG) or less massive AFP elevation.

   - Pros: Common testicular tumour in this age group, usually homogeneous solid mass.  
   - Cons: AFP is typically normal or slightly elevated (AFP is usually normal). High AFP and heterogeneous mass with cystic/necrotic areas argue against seminoma.

   - Pros: Can present with elevated beta-hCG rather than AFP.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.

There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass. When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged. It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment. An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations. <|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Incidental fat attenuation mass in the right ischiorectal fossa displacing the anal canal to the left.  
- Thin capsule surrounding the mass, no enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications on initial CT.  
- On follow-up CT five years later, significant enlargement of the mass with two cystic compartments and heterogeneous internal contents (fluid and soft tissue), with thin and thick septations, and irregular enhancing soft tissue rim.  
- MRI confirmed the heterogeneous nature of the central collection and thick, irregular rim enhancement.  
- The lesion is located in the ischiorectal fossa, right-sided, with fat attenuation and a capsule initially, then progressive growth and heterogeneous internal architecture with enhancement.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Extragonadal germ cell tumour: Typically solid, may have necrosis or cystic areas, but fat attenuation is not a characteristic feature. Usually in midline or para-aortic region, not ischiorectal fossa. Plus, the lesion's fat attenuation and location argues against this.  
2) Ischiorectal liposarcoma: Fat attenuation mass with septations and heterogeneous internal architecture and enhancement is typical. Capsular infiltration and progressive growth support malignancy. The location and imaging features are classic for a liposarcoma in the ischiorectal fossa.  
3) Dermoid cyst: Typically shows fat attenuation with possible calcifications (teeth, bone) and internal heterogeneity with sebaceous material. Usually midline or along midline sinuses, not typically ischiorectal fossa. No calcifications reported. However, fat component present, could be possible, but thick enhancing rim and growth is concerning.  
4) Tailgut cyst: Usually midline presacral cystic lesion arising from remnants of the tailgut, can be multicystic with septations. Typically not fat attenuation but fluid attenuation. Usually located retrorectally, not ischiorectal fat. Unlikely due to location and fat attenuation.  
5) Perineal epidermal cyst: Typically shows a well-defined cystic lesion, may have fat attenuation if ruptured or infected, but unusual to have septations or irregular enhancement. Usually small and stable. Enlargement and complex enhancement argue against simple epidermal cyst.  
6) Anterior sacral meningocele: CSF-filled sac herniating through a sacral defect, hypodense like CSF, no fat attenuation, no capsule or solid components, usually midline. Not fitting here.

C) Ranking:  
1) Ischiorectal liposarcoma: Matches location, fat attenuation mass, encapsulated with progressive growth and heterogeneous internal architecture with enhancement.  
2) Dermoid cyst: Fat content possible, but absence of calcifications and atypical location and growth pattern less likely.  
3) Tailgut cyst: Wrong location and imaging features.  
4) Perineal epidermal cyst: Unlikely due to complex enhancement and growth pattern.  
5) Extragonadal germ cell tumour and anterior sacral meningocele: Not fitting location or characteristic imaging.

Result: Ischiorectal liposarcoma best fits all key features.  
</analysis>

2) Ischiorectal liposarcoma: Fat attenuation mass with septations and heterogeneous internal architecture and enhancement is typical. Capsular infiltration and progressive growth support malignancy. The location and imaging features are classic for a liposarcoma in the ischiorectal fossa.  
3) Dermoid cyst: Typically shows fat attenuation with possible calcifications (teeth, bone) and internal heterogeneity with sebaceous material. However, fat component present, could be possible, but thick enhancing rim and growth is concerning.  
4) Tailgut cyst: Usually midline presacral cystic lesion arising from remnants of the tailgut, can be multicystic with septations. Typically not fat attenuation but fluid attenuation. Usually located retrorectally, not ischiorectal fat. Unlikely due to location and fat attenuation.

5) Extragonadal germ cell tumour and anterior sacral meningocele: Not fitting location or characteristic imaging.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

1) Spondylocostal dysostosis (JarchoâLevin syndrome):  
   - Pros: Presence of multiple rib and vertebral segmentation anomalies, bifid ribs, rib fusion, vertebral fusion, and scoliosis are classic for this diagnosis.  
   - Cons: Typically involves rib and vertebral segmentation defects but vertebral arch defects are less common. Volume loss can occur due to rib abnormalities.  

2) Spondylothoracic dysostosis (LavyâMoseley syndrome):  
   - Pros: Also presents with multiple vertebral segmentation anomalies and rib abnormalities, often severe thoracic insufficiency, and vertebral arch defects may be present.  
   - Cons: Usually shows a "crab" or "fan-like" configuration of the ribs rather than bifid or segmentally fused ribs; thoracic deformity is more pronounced. The presence of bifid ribs with widening and multiple rib fusions is more characteristic of spondylocostal dysostosis.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Spondylocostal dysostosis (JarchoâLevin syndrome): The bifid ribs, segmental fusion, and vertebral body fusion best match this diagnosis, with milder vertebral arch defects.  
2) Spondylothoracic dysostosis (LavyâMoseley syndrome): While some features overlap, the rib morphology and pattern of vertebral involvement are less typical for this syndrome.  

Thus, the findings of bifid ribs, multiple segmental rib fusions, vertebral element fusions, and mild posterior vertebral arch defects best align with spondylocostal dysostosis (JarchoâLevin syndrome).

</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

On the US exam, the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury, Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes, Acromioclavicular cyst, Soft-tissue tumour, Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear
FinalDiagnosis: Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear

  Pro: Soft tissue mass near ACJ could be hematoma or post-traumatic lesion.  
  Con: No history of trauma, no acute fracture, no signs of acute injury or hematoma; mass is rounded, homogeneous and fluid-like, not typical for traumatic lesion without trauma history.

  Pro: Osseous degenerative changes present at ACJ.  
  Con: Degenerative changes alone would not cause a large, rounded soft-tissue mass or cystic fluid collection; no mass formation typically.

  Con: None significant; findings fit well with this diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp (Figure 1).
 
 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp. Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing a “filarial dance” sign.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dirofilariasis, Epidermal inclusion cyst, Cysticercosis, Foreign body granuloma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 71-year-old woman with a left temporal scalp lesion.
- Thick-walled cystic lesion in subcutaneous plane of left temporal region.
- Ultrasound shows cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures.
- These mobile structures have echogenic walls and show a “filarial dance” sign.
- No trauma-related abnormality on CT.
- Clinical history includes recurrent swelling and itching at the same site for 6 months.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Dirofilariasis
   Pros: Dirofilariasis can present as a subcutaneous cystic lesion with live parasites seen on ultrasound ("filarial dance" sign). Mobile, tubular, echogenic structures are typical for live parasites such as Dirofilaria.
   Cons: Dirofilaria is a filarial parasite but usually transmitted by mosquito bites, often causing subcutaneous nodules, similar findings as described.

2) Epidermal inclusion cyst
   Pros: Common cystic lesion under the skin.
   Cons: Typically does not have mobile, echogenic, serpiginous structures on ultrasound; does not show "filarial dance" sign.

3) Cysticercosis
   Pros: Can present as cystic lesions with cysticerci inside.
   Cons: Usually shows a cystic lesion with an eccentric echogenic scolex, not mobile serpiginous structures with filarial dance sign; typically less than 1cm and in muscle or CNS; subcutaneous cysticercosis is less common and usually not with mobile structures.

4) Foreign body granuloma
   Pros: Can cause swelling and irritation.
   Cons: No imaging findings of foreign body or granulomatous reaction; no mobile structures; ultrasound findings do not fit.

C) Ranking:
1) Dirofilariasis â best matches ultrasound findings with live mobile parasites and filarial dance sign.
2) Cysticercosis â cystic lesion but no live mobile parasites seen.
3) Epidermal inclusion cyst â no live structures.
4) Foreign body granuloma â no evidence on imaging.

â¨Top candidate is Dirofilariasis due to pathognomonic filarial dance sign on ultrasound showing live moving echogenic structures consistent with Dirofilaria parasites. 
</analysis>

<final>Dirofilariasis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An
 
 orthopantomogram
 
 showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion (Figure 1). A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a
 
 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: An orthopantomogram showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion. A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus, compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis. During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table, also consistent with osteomas.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cemento-osseous dysplasia, Osteomatosis, Odontomas, Gardner’s syndrome, Hypercementosis, Hereditary multiple exostoses, Fibrous dysplasia

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- An 18-year-old female with persistent primary teeth (delayed tooth eruption).  
- Large exophytic sclerotic mass in the left mandibular ramus on orthopantomogram, described as ivory-like dense bone lesion consistent with osteoma.  
- Multiple smaller sclerotic lesions interspersed in mandible with cortical expansion.  
- Multiple well-delineated mature bone lesions interspersed among tooth roots, compatible with hypercementosis.  
- Palpable mass on left pre-auricular region, plus a mass on the forehead discovered on clinical exam.  
- Skull CT shows multiple small sessile sclerotic lesions on outer table of skull consistent with osteomas.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Cemento-osseous dysplasia  
  - Pros: Can present with sclerotic jaw lesions; mixed radiolucent-radiopaque lesions.  
  - Cons: Typically does not cause exophytic osteomas; does not explain osteomas on skull or pre-auricular mass; usually not associated with multiple osteomas or dental anomalies like persistent primary teeth.  

2) Osteomatosis  
  - Pros: Presence of multiple osteomas (jaw and skull).  
  - Cons: Osteomatosis is a rare entity; usually does not involve dental anomalies like persistent primary teeth; no mention of other associated findings like intestinal polyps. Also, osteomatosis alone does not explain hypercementosis and dental anomalies.  

3) Odontomas  
  - Pros: Radiopaque lesions in jaw.  
  - Cons: Odontomas are usually tumor-like lesions composed of dental tissue, usually well-circumscribed and do not cause exophytic bony osteomas on skull; do not explain multiple osteomas in craniofacial bones; no pre-auricular or skull osteomas typical.  

4) Gardnerâs syndrome  
  - Pros: Classic triad includes multiple osteomas of jaw and skull, impacted or persistent teeth, and hypercementosis; other features can include epidermoid cysts, intestinal polyps (not mentioned here but not required for diagnosis if other criteria met). This case perfectly matches the classic osteomas in mandible and skull plus dental anomalies like persistent primary teeth and hypercementosis.  
  - Cons: None apparent, findings fit very well.  

5) Hypercementosis  
  - Pros: Confirmed by imaging (mature bone lesions near tooth roots).  
  - Cons: Hypercementosis alone does not explain the exophytic osteomas in mandible and skull or the persistent primary teeth. Not sufficient for primary diagnosis.  

6) Hereditary multiple exostoses  
  - Pros: Multiple exophytic bone growths.  
  - Cons: Usually involve long bones of extremities; lesions are cartilaginous exostoses (exostoses), not osteomas; not typical for craniofacial bones or dental anomalies described.  

7) Fibrous dysplasia  
  - Pros: Can cause ground-glass sclerotic lesions in jaw; can cause deformities.  
  - Cons: Lesions usually have fuzzy margins, not well-delineated ivory-like exophytic osteomas; does not explain multiple skull osteomas or dental anomalies like persistent primary teeth.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Gardnerâs syndrome (fits all findings: multiple osteomas in jaw and skull, persistent primary teeth, hypercementosis).  
2) Osteomatosis (multiple osteomas but lacks dental anomalies).  
3) Cemento-osseous dysplasia (possible jaw lesions but no osteomas on skull).  

<final>Gardnerâs syndrome</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm
 
 (ap/axial/cc) (
 
 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (
 
 small arrow in Figure 2
 
 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-
 
 enhancing
 
 fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe (arrows on Figure 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm. Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level. Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-enhancing fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Retropharyngeal abscess, Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst, Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy, Suppurative thyroiditis
FinalDiagnosis: Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Nine-year-old female with fever, neck movement restriction, and posterior lateral left oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal bulge.
- Nasopharyngolaryngoscopy: lateral posterior wall bulge, no piriform sinus irregularities.
- Contrast-enhanced CT: well-defined oval fluid collection with rim enhancement in the retropharyngeal space (2x2x1 cm), with retropharyngeal space edema from skull base to hypopharynx.
- Thin, well-defined, rim-enhancing fistulous tract from inferior left border of retropharyngeal collection to upper pole of left thyroid lobe.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1) Retropharyngeal abscess:
   - Pros: Fluid collection with rim enhancement in retropharyngeal space with edema; clinical fever and neck restriction fit well.
   - Cons: The presence of a fistulous tract to the thyroid lobe is unusual and not typical for a simple abscess.

2) Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst:
   - Pros: These cysts are located along the neck and can be infected; fistulous tracts can be seen.
   - Cons: Retropharyngeal space is not a typical location for third or fourth branchial cleft cysts, which are usually lateral neck; also bilateral symmetrical retropharyngeal space collection less favored.

3) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy:
   - Pros: Necrotic nodes can mimic abscesses with rim enhancement.
   - Cons: No mention of enlarged lymph nodes; fistulous tract to thyroid lobe unusual; clinical presentation with bulging posterior oropharyngeal wall suggests a space-occupying lesion rather than lymphadenopathy.

4) Suppurative thyroiditis:
   - Pros: Fistulous tract ending in upper pole of left thyroid lobe suggests infection of thyroid.
   - Cons: Primary thyroid infection rarely causes a retropharyngeal collection; usual presentation focuses on thyroid swelling and pain; no evidence of thyroid involvement described except fistulous tract end.

C) Ranking and tie break:
- The fistulous tract connecting a retropharyngeal collection to the thyroid lobe suggests a process involving both spaces; suppurative thyroiditis rarely has a retropharyngeal collection.
- A retropharyngeal abscess is highly consistent with the primary fluid collection, rim enhancement, retropharyngeal edema, and clinical symptoms; the fistulous tract may represent spread of infection or fistulization.
- A branchial cleft cyst would be atypical in location and presentation.
- Necrotic adenopathy lacks supportive nodal findings.

Thus, the most fitting diagnosis is a retropharyngeal abscess with fistulous extension to the thyroid.

</analysis><final>Retropharyngeal abscess</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

- CEUS: No lesion enhancement.  
- MRI with hepatobiliary contrast: Lesion 1 (segment IVb) is T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular, with capsular retraction. Lesions 2 and 3 are T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity. No enhancement, no macroscopic fat, no diffusion restriction.  
- Normal biliary tree, no evidence of bile duct obstruction or macroscopic fat in lesions.  
- Prophylactic appendectomy and lesion biopsy (likely cutaneous lesion on the thigh) were performed during surgery.  
- Increasing size and infiltrative nature with capsular retraction on MRI.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:

   - Pros: Common pediatric liver tumor, hypervascular usually showing enhancement, can cause jaundice. Lesion size increase and infiltrative nature with capsular retraction fits.  
   - Cons: Typical hepatoblastomas enhance on CEUS/MRI; lack of enhancement and diffusion restriction argues against hepatoblastoma. No macroscopic fat (makes mixed hepatoblastoma less likely). Age fits, but enhancement missing.

   - Pros: Can present as hypovascular liver lesions, non-enhancing, no fat. Can be infiltrative. Occurs in young patients.  
   - Cons: Unusual presentation in neonates without systemic lymphoma signs. Typically multiple lesions, but capsular retraction less common. No diffusion restriction is unusual for lymphoma.

   - Pros: Associated with skin lesions (cutaneous lesion biopsied), commonly presents with subcutaneous or visceral lesions in neonates. Liver involvement with multiple lesions, no enhancement, hypointense T1 and hyperintense T2 is typical. Lesion morphology (infiltrative, capsular retraction) fits with fibrous tissue response. No fat, no diffusion restriction.  
   - Cons: Rare but plausible in neonates, especially with skin findings. No typical enhancement is consistent. Lesion behavior matches.  

4) Neuroblastoma  
   - Pros: Occurs in neonates, can metastasize to liver causing multiple lesions.  
   - Cons: Usually hypervascular with enhancement, often with calcifications or necrosis; can be heterogeneous. No enhancement on CEUS or MRI argues against. Lesions usually not hypovascular. No evidence of primary adrenal or paraspinal mass. No diffusion restriction unusual.

   - Pros: Common benign liver lesion in infants, can be multifocal. Typically T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense.  
   - Cons: Usually show characteristic enhancement on CEUS and MRI (peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in). Here, no enhancement is seen. The lesions are infiltrative with capsular retraction, which is unusual for hemangiomas (which are well-circumscribed).  

C) Ranking:  
1) Infantile myofibromatosis: Fits cutaneous lesion origin, liver lesions without enhancement, infiltrative with capsular retraction, T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, no fat or diffusion restriction.  
2) Lymphoma: Less likely due to age and missing diffusion restriction/enhancement.  
3) Hepatoblastoma: Typically enhances and shows diffusion restriction, which is absent.  
4) Neuroblastoma: No primary tumor seen, lesions atypical for neuroblastoma metastases.  
5) Haemangiomata: No enhancement and infiltrative capsular retraction argue against classic hemangioma appearance.  

<final> Infantile myofibromatosis </final><|return|>
options: Hepatoblastoma | Lymphoma | Infantile myofibromatosis | Neuroblastoma | Haemangiomata

 o’clock; m
 
 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.
 
 The abdominal CT scan with contrast (Figures 3a and 3b) shows a large heterogeneously enhancing mass in the retroperitoneal space with left kidney invasion and aortic wall encasement.
 
 The histopathologic exam reveals highly atypical spindle cells with large pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain.  Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an inferior glenohumeral ligament (IGHL) lesion. Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion. However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast. Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation. This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema. Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences, indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function. FinalDiagnosis: Humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligament (HALG injury) with superior labrum from anterior to posterior (SLAP) lesion

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.

1) Calcific tendinitis  
   - Pros: Can cause calcifications near joints.  
   - Cons: Usually focal, not extensive bilateral heterotopic mature ossification; no mention of tendon involvement; chronicity and large mature ossifications are atypical.  

2) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification  
   - Pros: Classic after traumatic brain injury, especially with prolonged immobilization; typically mature and extensive; can affect joints near the injury site (here, the shoulder); bilateral and extensive is typical; onset months after injury consistent.  
   - Cons: None significant.

   - Pros: Can cause heterotopic ossification in muscles after trauma or surgery.  
   - Cons: Usually unilateral, focal, localized to muscle trauma site; here, bilateral and extensive in joints; no history of direct muscular injury to shoulder.

   - Pros: Can cause calcifications or ossifications.  
   - Cons: Typically not extensive bilateral mature heterotopic bone formation in joints; no systemic or metabolic symptoms reported.  

5) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva  
   - Pros: Causes heterotopic ossification.  
   - Cons: Usually genetic, progressive from childhood; not acute post-traumatic bilateral shoulder ossification after TBI; no mention of pre-existing condition; onset age and history do not fit.

   - Pros: Causes periarticular calcifications, often bilateral.

7) Sarcoma

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).

Clinical History: A 4-month-old male infant presented with a palpable mass on his arm. The parents noticed it since he was a month and a half old. Clinical examination showed a painless mass without signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydronephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder. Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised. After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis. The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Post-transplant renal artery stent, Post-transplant renal vein stenting, Post-transplant ureteral stent removal, Post-transplant ureteral stent placement

   - Cons: Imaging shows an active stricture causing hydronephrosis; no mention of stent removal in the clinical scenario; nephrostomy placement suggests ongoing obstruction without relief from prior stent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 24-year-old female with accelerated hypertension and symptoms suggestive of catecholamine excess (paroxysmal palpitations, headache, giddiness).  
- Elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL), highly suggestive of catecholamine-secreting tumor.  
- Imaging shows a well-defined, heterogeneously hypervascular, hyperdense lesion in the right adnexa/paracervical region, distinct from the right ovary.  
- On CT, lesion shows intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement with central necrosis.  
- MRI shows heterogeneously iso to hyperintense T1, heterogeneously hyperintense T2 with "light bulb sign," and restricted diffusion.  
- 68Ga-DOTATATE scan shows intense homogeneous somatostatin receptor expression, SUVmax 20.19.  
- No invasion of adjacent structures; well-circumscribed mass.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Solitary Fibrous Tumor (SFT)  
- Pros: Hypervascular pelvic mass can be SFT; often lobulated and well-defined.  
- Cons: SFTs rarely secrete catecholamines; no typical elevation of normetanephrine; no intense DOTATATE uptake typical for neuroendocrine lesions.  
- Verdict: Unlikely given catecholamine excess and DOTATATE avidity.

2) Pelvic Paraganglioma  
- Pros: Paragangliomas secrete catecholamines (raised normetanephrine); intense DOTATATE uptake due to SSTR; hypervascular pelvic mass with necrosis is typical; "light bulb sign" hyperintense T2; well-circumscribed lesion; patient age and clinical presentation fits catecholamine excess.  
- Cons: None significant.

3) Hypervascular Soft Tissue Sarcoma  
- Pros: Hypervascular mass with necrosis possible; fits pelvic soft tissue tumor.  
- Cons: Does not secrete catecholamines; no significant DOTATATE uptake; clinical symptoms not typical; sarcomas usually more infiltrative.  

4) Hypervascular Lymphadenopathy (Metastases or Castleman Disease)  
- Pros: Hypervascular lymph nodes can show similar enhancement.  
- Cons: No evidence of primary malignancy; Castleman disease rare in this location; catecholamine excess not explained; no intense DOTATATE uptake typical for neuroendocrine tumor.  

5) Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumor (PNST)  
- Pros: Can be hypervascular; well-defined masses.  
- Cons: No catecholamine secretion; no significant DOTATATE avidity; clinical picture not suggestive.  

6) Aggressive Angiomyxoma  
- Pros: Typically occurs in pelvis in young women; hypervascular.  
- Cons: Usually non-enhancing or mildly enhancing; no catecholamine secretion; no significant DOTATATE uptake; MRI signal not typical "light bulb sign."  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pelvic Paraganglioma (pros outweigh cons; classical imaging, biochemical, and clinical correlation).  
2) Hypervascular Soft Tissue Sarcoma (second due to imaging but lacks neuroendocrine features).  
3) Solitary Fibrous Tumor (biochemical mismatch).  
4) Aggressive Angiomyxoma (clinical and imaging less suggestive).  
5) Hypervascular Lymphadenopathy (clinical and biochemical mismatch).  
6) Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumor (no biochemical support).  

The most convincing diagnosis is Pelvic Paraganglioma.  
</analysis>  
<final>Pelvic paraganglioma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).

A) Key findings:  
- 63-year-old woman with recurrent meningioma at the foramen magnum.  
- Preop MRI: extra-axial lesion 3.9 x 2.1 cm, compressing bulbus and upper spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and leftward, involving V4 segment of right vertebral artery.  
- Post-op day +2 MRI: persistent tumor; signal alterations in bulb and upper spinal cord: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, T1 hypointensity.  
- Diffusion sequences showed mixed vasogenic and cytotoxic edema suggesting ischemic-oedematous changes.  
- Clinically, immediate postoperative tetraplegia with preserved consciousness and need for ventilation.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Central cord syndrome:  
   - Pros: tetraplegia with sparing of finger function? Not described here.  
   - Cons: central cord syndrome typically results from hyperextension injury in older patients, not from tumor surgery or hypoperfusion; not associated with diffusely high T2/FLAIR signal and mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema in bulb and cord. Also, clinical scenario doesn't mention predilection for upper limbs.  

2) White cord syndrome:  
   - Pros: This rare syndrome occurs after decompressive cervical spine surgery. Characterized by sudden neurological deterioration with T2 hyperintensity on MRI reflecting edema (vasogenic and cytotoxic), no structural cause on imaging. It is thought due to reperfusion injury after sudden decompression. The timing (immediate postoperative tetraplegia, MRI changes with edema and no major compressive lesion) fits well.  
   - Cons: Usually described after cervical decompression; here lesion is at foramen magnum, but still upper cervical cord involved. "White cord syndrome" name derives from T2 hyperintensity ("white cord") pattern of edema. Mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema on diffusion-weighted imaging is consistent.  

3) Compressive myelopathy:  
   - Pros: preop tumor compressing spine and bulb. Could be present postoperatively if residual tumor compresses cord.  
   - Cons: Postop MRI shows partial tumor removal but also signal changes consistent with edema rather than only compression. Also, clinical deterioration immediate postoperative is abrupt and severe, not typical of gradual compressive myelopathy. Moreover, diffusion imaging shows mixed edema rather than only T2 signal changes expected in chronic compression.  

4) Epidural haematoma:  
   - Pros: Postoperative neurological deterioration could be explained by epidural haematoma compressing cord.  
   - Cons: No mention of haematoma or compressive lesion on MRI; instead, signal changes in cord with hyperintensity and edema. Also, imaging shows tumor persistence but no compressive haematoma.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- #1 White cord syndrome: best fits immediate postoperative tetraplegia, T2 hyperintensity, mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema without new compressive lesion, pathognomonic for reperfusion injury after decompression.  
- #2 Central cord syndrome: less likely given clinical context.  
- #3 Compressive myelopathy: less likely due to abrupt onset and imaging findings.  
- #4 Epidural haematoma: no imaging evidence of haematoma.  
Therefore, White cord syndrome is the leading diagnosis.  
</analysis>  
<final> White cord syndrome </final><|return|>

- #4 Epidural haematoma: no imaging evidence of haematoma.

OriginalDescription:

CT imaging also demonstrated the marked widening of the intercondylar notch (Figures 4 and 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of right breast pain and periareolar bruising associated with a 1-day periareolar indurated tumefaction. She denied any other symptoms, trauma, or regular medication use. Physical examination revealed a 2-cm indurated, painful cord-like structure in the periareolar region associated with bruising and petechial lesions.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the right breast revealed a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes along an extension of approximately 28 mm (Figures 1 and 2). No blood flow was detected on colour or spectral Doppler studies (Figure 3). Furthermore, the adjacent soft tissues showed increased echogenicity. No other anomalies were seen in the right breast. Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).
